exercises.prompt: How does a strong exposé begin?

options.text: With an accroche — a striking fact, question or scene
With « Alors, donc, le sujet est… »
With the conclusion, to save time

exercises.explanation: The hook comes first; examiners hear a hundred « alors donc » openings a day.

options.text: welcomes your own position and examples
forbids any personal opinion
must be read aloud word for word

exercises.explanation: The synthèse is objective; the exposé wants you to take position and argue it.

exercises.template: J'{{b1}} d'abord les causes, avant d'examiner les conséquences.
exercises.explanation: The plan is announced in the future: j'aborderai d'abord… avant d'examiner…
Venons-en à… = let us turn to… — the classic spoken transition.

exercises.prompt: Why write notes rather than a full script?

options.text: Examiners can hear reading — notes keep you speaking, not reciting
Scripts are forbidden in the exam room
Notes are easier to photocopy

exercises.explanation: Delivery counts: eye contact and natural speech beat a perfectly read script.

exercises.prompt: Close with a door left open (one word).
exercises.explanation: L'ouverture: end on a question that extends the topic — reste une question…

exercises.prompt: Match each exposé element to its role.
Put the words in order: "This brings me to my second point."
Listen to this exposé opening. Which two elements do you hear?
exercises.transcript: Un chiffre d'abord : huit jeunes sur dix vivent encore chez leurs parents à vingt ans. J'aborderai d'abord les causes économiques, avant d'examiner les mutations familiales.

options.text: An accroche (the striking figure) and the announced plan
A conclusion and an apology
Two unrelated anecdotes

exercises.explanation: Hook (un chiffre d'abord) + plan (j'aborderai… avant d'examiner…) — a model start.

exercises.prompt: Read these examiner notes and answer both questions.
exercises.passageFr: Candidat 14 — Accroche efficace (anecdote personnelle). Plan annoncé puis respecté ; transitions audibles (« venons-en à », « cela m'amène à »). Bémol : la conclusion ne dresse aucun bilan et s'arrête net, sans ouverture. Lecture des notes trop fréquente dans la deuxième partie.
exercises.passageEn: Candidate 14 — Effective hook (personal anecdote). Outline announced then followed; audible transitions ('venons-en à', 'cela m'amène à'). Weak point: the conclusion draws no summing-up and stops dead, with no opening-out. Read from notes too often in part two.

questions.prompt: What did the candidate do well?

options.text: Hook, announced plan, audible transitions
A perfect conclusion
Speaking without any notes
A bilan and an ouverture
A second accroche
More reading from notes

exercises.prompt: Why must transitions be AUDIBLE in an exposé?

options.text: The listener has no headings — your connectors are the headings
Examiners are usually far from the candidate
It fills time when you forget content

exercises.explanation: Spoken structure must be heard: clear connectors let the listener rebuild your plan.

exercises.prompt: Listen, then choose what it means.
exercises.transcript: J'aborderai d'abord les causes, avant d'examiner les conséquences.

options.text: I shall tackle the causes first, before examining the consequences.
Let us now come to the heart of the problem.
This brings me to my second point.
an oral presentation
a hook
to announce the outline

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word (from this unit's vocabulary).
exercises.explanation: « Son exposé a duré dix minutes. » — an oral presentation.

exercises.prompt: Match each word to its English meaning.
Put the words in order: “Let us now come to the heart of the problem.”
Speaking practice — listen, then say the sentence aloud and record yourself:
exercises.targetFr: J'aborderai d'abord les causes, avant d'examiner les conséquences.
exercises.targetEn: I shall tackle the causes first, before examining the consequences.

exercises.prompt: Speaking practice — say this sentence aloud and record yourself:
exercises.targetFr: Cela m'amène à mon second point.
exercises.targetEn: This brings me to my second point.
